summary:
Spherical alloys are preferable for dentistry due to improved manipulation during casting; they typically have a softer consistency that reduces stress when shaping compared to stiffer, traditionally machined counterparts which can lead to potential damage if mishandled. Agent2 reasoned by drawing an analogical parallel with material processing forces required under differing conditions, concluding that spherical alloys' inherent flexibility makes them safer and more efficient choices for precise dental operations without compromising strength upon setting. 

The summary has been condensed into exactly 2 sentences focusing only on clinically pertinent aspects related to the specific query about alloy type preferences based on procedural demands in prosthodontics. No extraneous details were included beyond what was necessary to address the core issue presented within the original problem statement concerning comparative advantages offered by each method across various scenarios encountered frequently among clinicians working closely with these substances daily.
